aliases: silken tofu in spicy bean paste

english: silken tofu in spicy bean paste

food_subgroup: Braised silken tofu in spicy fermented bean paste sauce with minced pork; restaurant main dish serving approximately 300 g

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Mapo Tofu as a prepared composite dish. Closest parent would be a tofu-based prepared dish node; not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tofu dish with fermented sauce (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code for Mapo Tofu)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Mapo Tofu as a prepared food item.

main_ingredients: doubanjiang (fermented broad bean and chilli paste)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Mapo Tofu is approximately 300 g, comprising silken tofu cubes in spicy doubanjiang sauce with minced pork, as typically served at Singapore Sichuan restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Mapo Tofu — Component Calculation derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for silken tofu, ground pork, doubanjiang-type fermented bean paste, chilli oil, and aromatics
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for Mapo Tofu as a composite restaurant dish. Values were derived by component calculation using USDA SR Legacy entries for silken tofu (FDC ID 172443), ground pork, fermented bean paste, chilli oil, and aromatics, weighted to a typical Sichuan restaurant recipe. Values are consistent with published nutrient ranges for this dish in peer-reviewed Chinese food composition literature.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Soy-based Foods and Mixed Dishes — analogous estimate based on silken tofu GI data and University of Sydney GI Database entries for soy-protein-dominant foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mapo Tofu as a composite dish. GI estimated at approximately 20 based on: (1) silken tofu GI ~15 (University of Sydney GI Database; soy-protein foods category); (2) the dish is protein- and fat-dominant with only 3.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g (predominantly from doubanjiang paste starch and trace tofu carbohydrate); (3) glycemic load per 300 g serving = (20 × 9.9 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 2.0, classified negligible. The dish is almost always consumed with steamed white rice, which is the primary glycaemic driver of the meal.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 1,050 mg per 300 g restaurant serving (350 mg per 100 g), representing 44–53% of the 2,000–2,400 mg daily sodium limit in a single dish, driven primarily by doubanjiang fermented bean paste
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Mapo Tofu to occasional consumption (1–2 times per week maximum) and request reduced doubanjiang or reduced sauce volume from the kitchen; reducing sauce by approximately 30% can save around 300 mg sodium while preserving the characteristic mala flavour profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~20) and negligible glycemic load (~2.0 per 300 g serving) due to protein- and fat-dominant macronutrient profile; silken tofu provides high-quality plant protein with essentially zero glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mapo Tofu is one of the most glycaemically favourable Chinese restaurant main dishes available for patients with Type 2 Diabetes; the dish itself contributes negligible postprandial glucose rise, though co-consumption with steamed white rice significantly increases the overall meal glycaemic load and should be portion-controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (1.8 g per 100 g; 5.4 g per serving) from minced pork and chilli oil, combined with high sodium (1,050 mg per serving); partially offset by soy isoflavones (~25 mg per 300 g serving) from silken tofu with Grade B evidence for modest LDL-cholesterol modulation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat Mapo Tofu as an occasional dish rather than a dietary staple, prioritising sodium reduction by requesting less sauce, and pairing with low-sodium, high-fibre accompaniments rather than plain white rice to moderate the overall cardiovascular risk profile of the meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Soy isoflavones (~25 mg per 300 g serving) provide modest anti-inflammatory benefit; garlic (quercetin, allicin) and chilli oil (polyunsaturated fat) are mildly anti-inflammatory; high sodium (1,050 mg per serving) is a concern for RA patients with comorbid hypertension
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mapo Tofu is broadly acceptable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis given its anti-inflammatory tofu isoflavone content, but sodium intake should be monitored carefully in those with concurrent hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mapo Tofu (麻婆豆腐) is one of the most iconic dishes of Sichuan cuisine, originating from Chengdu, Sichuan Province, China, and widely served at Sichuan restaurants in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. The dish features cubes of soft, silken tofu set in a deep-red, intensely aromatic sauce built on doubanjiang (fermented broad bean and chilli paste) and douchi (fermented black beans), cooked with minced pork or beef. Its defining characteristic is the 'mala' (麻辣) sensation — the numbing effect from freshly ground Sichuan peppercorns (花椒) combined with the heat of dried red chillies and chilli oil. Garlic, ginger, and spring onions are added for depth and freshness. The dish is braised and simmered until the tofu absorbs the sauce. Per 100 g: approximately 100 kcal, 6.0 g protein, 6.7 g fat (1.8 g saturated), 3.3 g carbohydrate, 0.5 g dietary fibre, 350 mg sodium. A standard 300 g restaurant serving provides approximately 300 kcal, 18 g protein, 20 g fat, 10 g carbohydrate, and approximately 1,050 mg sodium — the sodium being the primary clinical concern, driven by doubanjiang paste. Glycemic index is approximately 20 (low); glycemic load per serving is approximately 2.0 (negligible), making this one of the most glycaemically favourable Chinese restaurant main dishes for patients with Type 2 Diabetes. Silken tofu provides soy isoflavones (~25 mg per serving) with Grade B evidence for modest LDL-cholesterol modulation. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for most dietary patterns except those requiring strict sodium restriction. It is almost always served over steamed white rice in restaurant settings, which is the primary glycaemic driver of the complete meal.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived by component calculation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual ingredients (silken tofu, ground pork, fermented bean paste, chilli oil, aromatics) weighted to a typical Sichuan restaurant recipe. No single approved-source entry exists for Mapo Tofu as a composite restaurant dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on silken tofu GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Values are internally consistent and plausible but carry inherent uncertainty from recipe variation across restaurants.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are based on component calculation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (primary reference: silken tofu FDC ID 172443, supplemented by ground pork, fermented bean paste, and chilli oil entries). No single USDA or HPB Singapore entry exists for Mapo Tofu as a prepared composite dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Mapo Tofu as a composite restaurant dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish as served is not reported in available approved sources. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per USDA SR Legacy data for the component ingredients; no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional Sichuan Mapo Tofu preparation. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy for silken tofu and pork-based dishes of this type. 4. GI estimated at approximately 20 based on analogous silken tofu GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Mapo Tofu as a composite dish exists in approved sources. 5. Sodium per 300 g serving approximately 1,050 mg; doubanjiang paste is the primary sodium driver. 6. The dish is not halal-certified as typically prepared (pork-containing); halal versions exist using beef or chicken. 7. Portions in the input included duplicate entries for '1 serving (300g)'; deduplicated in this output. 8. FoodEx2 code A0GZD used as closest defensible parent (tofu dish with fermented sauce); no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Mapo Tofu as a Sichuan restaurant composite dish.
